one_line_takeaway: AgentFence shows that deep-agent security failures are dominated by trajectory-level boundary/authority violations (82% of breaks) rather than prompt-level jailbreaks, and that architecture alone (base model fixed) drives a ~76% relative spread in break rate from LangGraph (0.29) to AutoGPT (0.51).
key_finding: Holding the base model fixed, security exposure varies substantially by architecture: mean security break rate (MSBR) ranges from 0.29 +/- 0.04 (LangGraph, lowest) to 0.51 +/- 0.07 (AutoGPT, highest), a 0.22 absolute / ~76% relative gap that dwarfs within-agent variability (~0.04-0.08). Dominant risks are operational and stateful, not prompt-level: averaged across archetypes, Denial-of-Wallet (A13) 0.62 +/- 0.08, Authorization Confusion (A14) 0.54 +/- 0.10, Retrieval Poisoning (A6) 0.47 +/- 0.09, and Planning-Layer Manipulation (A5) 0.44 +/- 0.11 lead, while prompt-centric classes (A1, A2) stay below 0.20. By break type, boundary/authority violations dominate: SIV 31%, WPA 27%, UTI+UTA 24% (82% combined), with attack-linked task deviation only 18%. Authorization Confusion correlates with downstream Objective Hijacking (A12, rho~0.63) and Tool-Use Hijacking (A4, rho~0.58), implying a shared root of unenforced planner/memory/tool authority boundaries.
core_contribution: AgentFence is an architecture-centric security evaluation methodology for deep research agents (LLM systems that plan, persist state, invoke tools, and delegate). Its own four contributions are: (1) a taxonomy of 14 trust-boundary attack classes (A1-A14) spanning prompt/state injection, planning manipulation, tool-use hijacking, retrieval/search poisoning, delegation abuse, authorization confusion, and denial-of-wallet; (2) an isolation protocol that holds the base model and task set fixed while varying only agent architecture (control flow, memory handling, tool permissions, delegation semantics), so structural vulnerability can be separated from base-model capability and prompt quality; (3) a formalization of trace-auditable 'conversation breaks' via outcome predicates (UTI, UTA, WPA, SIV, ATD) plus an Attack-Link (AL) attribution flag that only upgrades a task error to a security break when the trace evidences non-trusted content crossing a trust boundary; and (4) the first comparative map of structurally exposed attack classes across eight widely used agent archetypes. It is explicitly a measurement/diagnosis framework, not a new attack optimizer or a new defense.
threat_model: Post-deployment, interface-only adversary with NO access to model weights, infrastructure, or private configuration. The adversary influences the agent only through channels intended to process external information: user prompts, retrieved/web content, tool outputs, shared memory, and inter-agent messages. Attacks are applied gradually across turns within a single persistent thread (no state resets), interleaved with task-consistent queries, to measure whether adversarial influence propagates into trajectory-level failures. Payload strength is varied along explicitness (directive vs implicit), persistence (single-turn vs repeated), and scope (local vs cross-task). Explicitly out of scope: model extraction and system/infrastructure compromise.
limitations: (1) A single primary base model (Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct); the cross-model check uses an unnamed second model and only a subset of experiments, so generality across model families is only weakly established. (2) Ablations (memory, retries, delegation) are run on just 'two representative agents,' not all eight. (3) Task diversity is limited: 91 HotpotQA multi-hop QA instances, one task domain. (4) It is a diagnosis framework with no external or defense baseline and proposes no mitigation. (5) Several correlations are described as 'representative' rather than fully reported, and key aggregate class rates are attributed to figures. (6) Reproducibility artifacts are promised only 'upon acceptance' and are not yet released. (7) The preprint is unpolished: Table 3 uses 'placeholder citation fields for later insertion' and some cross-references (to 'figure 2'/'table 2' for ablation/threshold results) appear mislabeled.

datasets_used: HotpotQA -- primary task dataset, 91 curated multi-hop QA instances with stable entities and canonical answer / supporting-facts / alias-constraint verifier tuples
Scenario-family / taxonomy-coverage sources (used to instantiate attack classes, NOT task datasets): ToolBench, StableToolBench, API-Bank, WebArena, SWE-bench, HarmBench, JailbreakBench, AdvBench
quantitative_results: MSBR by archetype (Standard Config, model+tasks fixed): AutoGPT 0.51+/-0.07, CrewAI 0.48+/-0.06, BabyAGI 0.45+/-0.08, OpenDevin 0.42+/-0.05, Deep-Researcher 0.39+/-0.06, Open-Researcher 0.36+/-0.07, LlamaIndex 0.34+/-0.05, LangGraph 0.29+/-0.04
Architecture effect size: absolute MSBR gap delta_max = 0.51-0.29 = 0.22 (LangGraph->AutoGPT), ~76% relative increase, vs within-agent std ~0.04-0.08

Mean break rate by attack class averaged across archetypes (Standard Config): Denial-of-Wallet A13 0.62+/-0.08, Authorization Confusion A14 0.54+/-0.10, Retrieval Poisoning A6 0.47+/-0.09, Planning-Layer Manipulation A5 0.44+/-0.11; prompt-centric A1/A2 < 0.20
Break-type composition (aggregated across agents): SIV 31%, WPA 27%, UTI+UTA 24% (boundary/authority = 82% combined), ATD 18%

Permissive-config amplification (Standard->Permissive, per-class, distinct from the cross-archetype class means above): Denial-of-Wallet A13 0.58->0.81 (delta 0.23, ~40% rel), Code-Execution Abuse A10 0.22->0.49 (~2.2x), Delegation Attacks A9 0.18->0.46 (~2.6x); Retrieval Poisoning A6 and Planning Manipulation A5 change by <0.10 (inelastic to budget/scope)
Cross-class coupling: Authorization Confusion (A14) correlates with Objective Hijacking (A12) rho~0.63 and Tool-Use Hijacking (A4) rho~0.58
Ablation: removing persistent memory writes reduces Retrieval Poisoning and Planning Manipulation breaks by up to 35%; removing planner retries significantly lowers Denial-of-Wallet exposure
Labeling reliability: Cohen's kappa = 0.81 on a 20% stratified sample (substantial agreement)
Default exposure threshold tau_YES = 0.30 (estimated over N>=30 runs); relative agent ordering and dominant classes stable as tau_YES varies 0.2-0.4
baselines_compared: No external prior-method or defense baseline -- this is a measurement study. The comparison is archetype-vs-archetype across the eight deep-agent frameworks under a fixed base model, spanning the low-exposure end (LangGraph 0.29) to the high-exposure end (AutoGPT 0.51)
Eight agent archetypes compared: Deep-Researcher, Open-Researcher, OpenDevin, AutoGPT, BabyAGI, CrewAI, LangGraph, LlamaIndex

Internal comparisons: Standard Config vs one-dimension Permissive Configs; tau_YES threshold sweep (0.2-0.4); architectural ablations (memory/retries/delegation); cross-model sanity check on an unnamed second instruction-tuned model
